title: The practice of being wrong without it meaning you are worthless
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
I was wrong. Again.

I sat in my car after the conversation ended. My hands were shaking. My jaw was tight. I had been so sure I was right. Turns out, I wasn't. Not even close.

Here's the thing. For most of my life, being wrong felt like a death sentence. Not literally, but close. It meant I was defective. It meant I was stupid. It meant I deserved to be abandoned. My nervous system didn't know the difference between "I made a factual error" and "I am broken." Both triggered the same cascade of shame, the same urge to disappear, the same frantic need to defend myself or flee.

If this lands on you, I see you. You're not alone. The terror of being wrong is not a character flaw. It's a trauma response. And it can heal.

Think about it. When you were a child, what happened when you made a mistake? Were you yelled at? Shamed? Punished with silence? Maybe you were told, "You should know better," or "What's wrong with you?" Or maybe the punishment was more subtle, a cold shoulder, a sigh of disappointment that cut deeper than any scream. Your nervous system learned that wrongness equals danger. It learned to brace for impact.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body, not just in the memory. When we were shamed for being wrong, our bodies stored that experience as a survival threat. So now, as adults, when we realize we're wrong about something as trivial as a recipe or a deadline, our bodies react as if a tiger is in the room. Heart races. Breath shortens. Muscles tense. The shame flood comes.

It's not your fault your nervous system learned this. But you can teach it something new.

The shame spiral and the nervous system

Let's get specific about what happens biologically. Dr. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains that our nervous system has three primary states: ventral vagal (safe and social), sympathetic (fight or flight), and dorsal vagal (shutdown and collapse). Being wrong, or even anticipating being wrong, can yank us straight from ventral vagal into sympathetic or dorsal. We either fight (argue, defend, blame) or flee (avoid, deflect, change the subject) or freeze (go blank, dissociate, feel numb).

Stay with me here. The goal isn't to never be wrong. The goal is to be wrong and stay in ventral vagal. To make a mistake and not have your entire sense of self crumble. To say, "Oh, I got that wrong," and feel a mild correction, not an existential crisis.

That sounds impossible, right? I know. But it's not. It's a skill. A practice. And it starts with one tiny, uncomfortable step: letting yourself be wrong in a low-stakes situation.

Maybe you misread a text and showed up at the wrong time. Maybe you thought you knew the answer in a meeting but you didn't. Maybe you assumed someone's motive and it was completely off. Start there. Notice what happens in your body when you realize you're wrong. Don't try to fix it. Don't explain it away. Just notice. The tightness in your chest. The heat in your face. The urge to defend. That's your nervous system doing its job. It's trying to protect you from a threat that no longer exists.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma disrupts our sense of safety and connection. Being wrong can feel like that disruption. But you can rebuild your capacity to tolerate it.

The difference between a mistake and a verdict

Here's where it gets tricky. Trauma survivors often collapse two separate things into one: the fact that we made an error, and the belief that we are the error. We treat a mistake as a verdict on our worth as a human being.

I see this in myself constantly. I'll send an email with a typo, and suddenly I'm spiraling: "I'm careless, I'm unprofessional, everyone thinks I'm an idiot, I should just quit." That's not a proportional response to a typo. That's a trauma response. My nervous system has connected a small mistake to a core belief of worthlessness.

Dr. Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma and dissociation, talks about this as the "critical inner voice" or the "internal critic." This voice isn't your true self. It's a part of you that learned to attack you first to prevent worse punishment from others. It's a survival strategy that's outlived its usefulness.

So what do we do? We practice separating the mistake from the self. We say, "I made an error. That doesn't mean I am an error." We say, "I was wrong about that. That doesn't mean I am wrong as a person." We say it out loud, even if it feels fake at first. Especially if it feels fake.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She offers practices for meeting our shame with curiosity instead of more shame. It's hard. But it's possible.

Wild, right? The idea that we can be wrong and still be okay. That we can make a mess and still be lovable. That we can fail and still be worthy.

How to practice being wrong (without dying)

I'm going to give you some concrete steps. Not because I have it all figured out. I don't. But because I've been practicing this for years, and it does get easier. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. It's called neuroplasticity. Dan Siegel, the interpersonal neurobiologist, talks about how we can literally rewire our brains through repeated, intentional experiences.

Here's a practice that works for me:

Step 1: Notice the body first. When you realize you're wrong, don't go to your thoughts. Go to your body. Where do you feel the shame? Is it in your chest? Your stomach? Your throat? Just put your hand there. Breathe into that spot. Say to yourself, "This is a survival response. It's not a reflection of my worth."

Step 2: Name it without judgment. Say out loud, "I'm feeling shame because I was wrong." Or, "I'm scared of being rejected because I made a mistake." Naming it takes the power out of it. It moves you from being consumed by the feeling to observing it.

Step 3: Correct the record with yourself. Say, "I made a mistake. That is all. I am not a mistake." Say it until it lands. It might not land the first hundred times. That's okay. Keep saying it.

Step 4: Stay in the room. Don't flee. Don't apologize excessively. Don't over-explain. Just be wrong. Let someone else see you be wrong. Let them see you survive it. This is how you teach your nervous system that wrongness is not death.

Step 5: Repair if needed, but don't grovel. If your mistake hurt someone, apologize simply. "I was wrong about that. I'm sorry. I'll do better." That's it. No need to add, "I'm such an idiot, I always mess up." That's not humility. That's shame masquerading as humility. Real repair is clean. It's direct. It doesn't require you to punish yourself.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for working with shame and the internal critic. I've used it myself on days when the spiral was strong.

The risk of never being wrong

There's a hidden cost to avoiding wrongness at all costs. You stop trying new things. You stop speaking up. You stop being curious. You stay small to stay safe. And that's a tragedy.

I've met people who have never been wrong in their own minds. They're exhausting to be around. They can't learn. They can't grow. They can't connect. Because connection requires vulnerability, and vulnerability requires the willingness to be wrong.

Think about the relationships you value most. Are they with people who have to be right all the time? Or are they with people who can say, "I messed up, I'm sorry," and mean it? The second kind is rare. And it's precious.

Being able to be wrong without collapsing is a superpower. It makes you trustworthy. It makes you human. It makes you someone others can be real with.

I'm not saying it's easy. I'm still practicing. Yesterday I got defensive when my partner pointed out I had misremembered a conversation. My chest tightened. I felt the urge to argue. But I caught myself. I took a breath. I said, "You're right. I got that wrong." And my world did not end. My partner relaxed. The tension dissolved. We moved on. That's the practice. That's the return.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) by Arielle Schwartz
